title: Unit 13 Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass: Direct and Indirect Object Pronouns (Weak Pronouns)

script.text: Welcome... to the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 13: Direct and Indirect Object Pronouns. In Unit 3, we learned the subject pronouns like

and how Greeks usually drop them. But today... we meet the real stars of everyday conversation. The little words for 'me'... 'him'... or 'it'. We call them Weak Pronouns. I'm William... and here with me is Athina. Athina... why do we call them 'weak'?

We call them weak because they are short... unstressed... and they cannot stand alone. They are like parasites... they must cling to a verb to survive. But before we use them... we must know if the person is the 'Direct Target' or the 'Indirect Receiver'.

This is the big distinction. If I say 'Do you see me?'... I am the direct target. That's the Accusative case. But if I say 'Give the coffee TO me'... I am the receiver. In Greek... you don't need the word 'to'... you just use the Genitive case. Athina... show us the difference between 'Me' and 'To Me'.
One vowel change... and the whole meaning shifts. But wait... what about 'Us' and 'You all'?
These are our favorites! They are 'Freebies'.
means 'You all' and 'To You all'. No need to change anything!
I love a freebie. Now... let's talk about the 'Clingy Rule'. Where do these pronouns sit in a sentence? In English... they come after the verb. 'I see HIM'. But in Greek?
They must be first. And if you use particles like
for no... the pronoun jumps right in the middle.
I will tell you. But wait... there is a huge 'Command Exception' in our lesson. If I’m giving an order... like 'Tell me!'... what happens?
If it is a command... the pronoun is forced to move to the very END of the verb. It becomes a tail!
for a command. Tell it to me!
From front to back. And what about 'Stacking'? If I want to say 'I give it to her'... I need two pronouns. What is the Greek order of operations?
Greek is very polite: the Person always comes before the Thing. Indirect before Direct.
for her comes first. And a native secret: if you say
for 'to you it'... we blend it into a single sharp sound.

I am telling it to you. You have now completed the exhaustive Masterclass for Unit 13. You can target people... receive gifts... stack pronouns like a pro... and even handle the command exceptions. Review the shopping and clothes vocabulary to practice these object pronouns. Excellent work... we’ll see you in Unit 14... where we dive into the Past.
Συγχαρητήρια! Τα λέμε στο επόμενο μάθημα.
